metadata.title: Agentic Research Course
metadata.author: Seyed Yahya Shirazi, Ph.D.
metadata.description: Week 3: Project Management with AI -- Structured Definitions, Epics, Worktrees, and Checks

elements.content: # Agentic Research Course
Week 3: Project Management with AI

**Seyed Yahya Shirazi, Ph.D.**
Assistant Project Scientist, Swartz Center for Computational Neuroscience
UC San Diego

[Open Science Collective](https://osc.earth)
Course: [courses.osc.earth/agentic-research](https://courses.osc.earth/agentic-research/) | Discord: [discord.gg/3JtBrU3c](https://discord.gg/3JtBrU3c)

slides.speakerNotes:
- Welcome back

- Last week: Claude Code setup (install, CLAUDE.md, plan mode)

- Today: how to organize real work so the agent stays trustworthy at research scale

- Walkthrough format: ~30 min presentation, then we build a real repo live

- Course site and Discord on the slide

elements.content: ## Where We Are

items.text: **Week 1 -- Git and GitHub.** The safety net. Nothing you do with AI is safe without it.
**Week 2 -- Claude Code.** The agent. Reads your project, plans, writes, runs, iterates.
**Today -- Project management.** Structure that lets a research-scale project stay coherent.

elements.content: A one-off script is a commit. A study-level analysis is a project. The bigger the project, the more structure pays.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 4x to reveal fragments]

- Week 1 gave you the safety net (git + GitHub)

- Week 2 gave you the agent (Claude Code)

- The agent without structure will happily produce code you cannot trust

- Today fixes that

elements.content: ## AI Is Powerful. Structure Turns Power Into Results.

### Unstructured

- "Can you analyze this EEG data?"
- Agent writes 400 lines; you cannot audit them
- Speed becomes technical debt
- Every output needs a full re-review
- You lose trust in the code

### Structured

- Goal, rough steps, checks, success criteria written down
- Agent plans phase-by-phase; you reshape the plan
- Each phase has a failure mode and a PR review
- Speed becomes output
- You extend trust to the pipeline
Same agent. Same prompt budget. The difference is the **steps + checks + balances** you put in front of it.

slides.speakerNotes:
- The thesis slide. Do not rush.

- Same model, same prompt budget. Unstructured vs structured is the delta.

- 'Speed becomes technical debt' vs 'speed becomes output'

- Transition: so where does structure start? With how you describe the problem.

elements.content: ## The Most Undervalued Artifact: A Structured Problem Definition

elements.alt: Markdown document with five sections: Goal, Steps, Checks, Success, QC

items.text: **Goal** -- one or two sentences. What question does this answer?
**Rough steps** -- a phase sketch the plan can renegotiate. Not a prescription.
**Checks** -- what would invalidate the analysis? Name the failure modes up front.
**Success** -- what does "done" look like? A figure, a p-value, a saved STUDY file.
**Quality control** -- data-quality notes, gotchas, drift filters, invalidation rules.

elements.content: Conversational works. Markdown is better. Markdown is versioned; Claude reads it every session; humans can review it.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 6x to reveal fragments]

- Most important teaching slide. Spend 90 seconds here.

- Read the five labels aloud.

- Concrete example is coming on slide 7. Our `project_brief.md` has all five sections.

- Scope matters: project-level (project_brief.md) for the project; user-level CLAUDE.md for defaults that apply to every project.

elements.content: ## `/project:init-project` -- Scaffold Standing Files

elements.code:
your-project/
├── CLAUDE.md     # project-scope AI context, goals, rules
├── .rules/       # team standards (git, docs, tests)
├── .context/     # plan.md, ideas.md, research.md, scratch_history.md
└── .gitignore

elements.content: **Local scaffold today.** Remote CI/CD (pre-commit hooks, GitHub Actions, linters) arrives in Week 4.

slides.speakerNotes:
- init-project is in the `research-skills` plugin

- CLAUDE.md: project-scope AI context

- .rules/: team standards (git conventions, docs, review process, testing policy)

- .context/: four working-state files (plan, ideas, research, scratch_history)

- This is everything Claude Code reads on startup in this project

- Today everything is local; Week 4 adds remote automated gates

elements.content: ## Epic = Multi-Phase Feature
A feature too large for one PR. Decompose into phases; each phase ships independently.

items.text: **Phase** = one reviewable, mergeable slice of the epic
**Sub-issue** = GitHub Issue linked to the parent epic issue
**Branch** = feature branch scoped to one phase
**Worktree** = separate working directory for that branch
**PR** = the review gate before the phase merges back to the epic branch

elements.content: Phase = sub-issue = branch = worktree = PR. One unit of work, five artifacts that hold each other accountable.

slides.speakerNotes:
- Epic terminology borrowed from agile; applies naturally to research projects

- Phase = sub-issue = branch = worktree = PR. Say this line three times if you have to.

- Each phase holds the others accountable: you cannot merge without PR; PR needs sub-issue; sub-issue needs branch; branch runs in its worktree

elements.content: ## Our Epic -- HBN "The Present" Boy vs Puppy ERSP

### Research Question

Do the first 500 ms of EEG differ when a shot opens with the boy visible vs the puppy visible?

### Dataset

- HBN-EEG Release 3 (ds005507)
- Task: `ThePresent` (3.5-min Pixar short)
- 183 subjects, 128-channel EEG, BIDS
- Sampling rate: 500 Hz (mini: 100 Hz)

### Tools

- EEGLAB 2024+ (AMICA, IClabel, dipfit5, std_precomp)
- `matlab-mcp-tools` so Claude drives MATLAB

### In Hand

- `project_brief.md` (structured problem definition)
- `shot_events.tsv` (event table)

### Shot Stats (trusted labels)

### Generated Live

`CLAUDE.md`, `.rules/`, `.context/`, epic + six sub-issues, Phase 1 MATLAB

slides.speakerNotes:
- First time the audience sees a concrete project. Keep this slide up.

- Two files in hand; everything else gets generated during the walkthrough

- 49 trusted shots = plenty of trials per ERSP condition

- The drift filter (>1.0s diff) invalidated 3 rows where the nearest annotation came from a different shot

- Tools: EEGLAB + matlab-mcp (the MCP server that lets Claude Code drive MATLAB)

elements.content: ## Six Phases, Six Checkpoints

elements.alt: Git timeline: main at top; epic branch below; six phase branches merge into epic, which carries an integration checkpoint before merging back to main

items.text: **Phase 1.** Preprocess R3 -- import BIDS, high-pass 1 Hz, cleanline 60/120/180 Hz, channel rejection
**Phase 2.** AMICA + dipfit -- per-subject ICA, attach weights, compute dipoles
**Phase 3.** IClabel -- classify components, keep brain, drop the rest
**Phase 4.** Shot events + epoch -- expand `shot_events.tsv`, epoch around shots, split by flag
**Phase 5.** ERSP precompute + IC clustering -- study-level, 3-80 Hz
**Phase 6.** Group statistics + figures -- cluster contrasts, manuscript-ready plots

elements.content: **Phases merge into the epic branch first, not `main`.** The epic branch acts as an integration checkpoint: once all six phases are merged there you get one combined test before the epic PR goes to `main`. On simpler projects you can skip the epic branch; on research pipelines with six phases it is worth the extra review.

slides.speakerNotes:
- Phases are not immutable. /plan will renegotiate them.

- Each phase has a natural failure mode. Preprocess: bad channels not removed. AMICA: non-convergence. IClabel: too few brain components. Epoch: event count mismatch. ERSP: zero-trial conditions. Stats: multiple-comparison blowup.

- The epic branch is the integration point. On involved projects phases merge INTO the epic branch first, then the whole epic merges to main as one PR. You get a combined integration test that none of the individual phase PRs can give you.

- Reference pipeline in study_handy_scripts.m is a known-working recipe. Use it; do not copy it verbatim.

elements.content: ## GitHub Issues + Sub-Issues
Parent issue describes the epic. One sub-issue per phase, linked to the parent. Sub-issue status = phase status.

items.text: Install once: `gh extension install agbiotech/gh-sub-issue`
Parent epic: `gh issue create --title "Epic: ..." --label epic`  -> issue `#1`
Phase 1: `gh issue create --title "Phase 1: Preprocess R3" --label feature`  -> issue `#2`
Link them: `gh sub-issue add 1 --sub-issue-number 2`
Repeat for phases 2..6

elements.content: No out-of-band trackers. Issues live where the code lives; PRs close them automatically.

slides.speakerNotes:
- gh sub-issue is an extension; install once per machine

- Parent epic issue describes the goal and lists phase sub-issues

- Each phase sub-issue tracks one branch and one PR

- Sub-issue status (open/closed) = phase status

- You can see the whole epic tree from the parent issue page on GitHub

elements.content: ## Git Worktrees -- Parallel Phase Development

elements.alt: main folder branching to epic-hbn, hbn-phase1, hbn-phase2 folders

items.text: **Epic branch:** `git worktree add ../epic-hbn -b feature/epic main`
**Phase 1:** `git worktree add ../hbn-phase1 -b feature/p1 feature/epic`
**Phase 2, in parallel:** `git worktree add ../hbn-phase2 -b feature/p2 feature/epic`
**Clean up after merge:** `git worktree remove ../hbn-phase1`

slides.speakerNotes:
- Worktrees solve context loss, not a feature

- No stashing, no git stash pop, no switching branches in the same directory

- Claude Code runs fine in each worktree; point it at the directory of the phase you want to work on

- Clean up with `git worktree remove` after the phase PR merges

elements.content: ## `/plan` -- A Check on Your Reasoning

items.text: Plan mode proposes files, functions, tests, and open questions **before** any code is written
You reshape the plan -- reorder steps, drop items, add checks, pin parameter values
Only after you approve does Claude start implementing
Enter with Shift+Tab until the status line shows "plan mode"

elements.content: Self-check: is the problem defined tightly enough for the plan to be short? Long plan = thin problem definition.

slides.speakerNotes:
- /plan is the single most leveraged habit in the course

- Frame it as a check on your reasoning: if your definition is weak, the plan will be sprawling and generic

- If the plan is short and specific, your definition was solid

- Reshape the plan before any code gets written. Cheap to edit text, expensive to refactor code.

elements.content: ## `/review-pr` -- A Check on the Output

elements.alt: Central PR card with six reviewer tokens around it

items.text: **code-reviewer** -- style, naming, logic, OWASP-class issues
**silent-failure-hunter** -- try/except swallowing errors, fallbacks hiding bugs
**comment-analyzer** -- stale, wrong, or load-bearing comments
**pr-test-analyzer** -- does test coverage match the feature?
**type-design-analyzer** -- invariants, encapsulation, enforcement
**code-simplifier** -- removes duplication, tightens the diff

elements.content: Address every finding. Skip only genuine false positives and explain why in the PR. No technical debt carried forward.

slides.speakerNotes:
- AI reviewing AI, supervised by you

- Each reviewer has its own lens; together they catch what a single review misses

- 'No tech debt carried forward' is the rule. Skipping a finding = explain it in the PR thread.

- This is the check-on-output. Pairs with /plan (check on reasoning) and shot_events.tsv (check on data quality).

elements.content: ## Week 4 Adds Automated Checks. Today Already Ships.

items.text: **Week 4 adds** -- pre-commit hooks (ruff, biome), GitHub Actions, type checkers (ty, pyright), typo linters
**Those are automated checks** -- they run without you having to remember
**Today's workflow already ships** -- structured definition + `/plan` + `/review-pr` is enough to produce trustworthy research code
**CI/CD is leverage, not prerequisite** -- do not wait to learn it before starting your project

elements.content: If you walk out of today with only the epic + plan + review-pr loop, you can ship research code.

slides.speakerNotes:
- Common failure mode: people think they need CI/CD before starting. They do not.

- Today's workflow is already enough to produce trustworthy code

- CI/CD next week adds automated gates; they are leverage on top of a working workflow

- This is the bridge slide into the walkthrough

elements.content: ## Walkthrough Roadmap -- Next ~30 Minutes, Live

items.text: `git init` the practicum locally
Copy `project_brief.md` + `shot_events.tsv` into the new repo
Run `/project:init-project` -- scaffold `CLAUDE.md`, `.rules/`, `.context/`
Push a **fresh private** repo to `OpenScience-Collective/agentic-research-practicum`
Create the epic issue and six phase sub-issues
`/plan` Phase 1 preprocessing -- reshape the plan before any code
Approve the plan; watch Claude write the first MATLAB function via `matlab-mcp`

slides.speakerNotes:
- Read the seven steps. Do not do them here; that is the walkthrough.

- Audience needs to see the arc before we start typing

- Transition: final slide stays on screen while we run step 1

elements.content: ## Questions? Ask While the Agent Preprocesses.

### What We Have

**`project_brief.md`**

- Goal
- Rough steps
- Checks
- Success criteria
- Quality control

**`shot_events.tsv`**

- 56 shots
- `has_boy`, `has_puppy`
- `LLR`, `match_diff_s`
- 49 trusted rows after drift filter

### What We Do Next, Live

1. Init practicum locally
2. Push private to `OpenScience-Collective`
3. Create epic + six sub-issues
4. `/plan` Phase 1
5. Implement Phase 1 preprocessing for HBN Release 3 in MATLAB/EEGLAB via `matlab-mcp`
